title: Trinidadian corn soup
subtitle: Thick roadside corn and split pea soup with dumplings
excerpt: A thick, sustaining Trinidadian street soup of corn on the cob, yellow split peas, potato, and small flour dumplings in a scotch bonnet and thyme broth. The Sunday morning road-side staple.
sourceNotes: Trinidadian corn soup is sold from large aluminium pots at roadside vendors, parties, and late-night spots across Trinidad. The soup is thick with split peas and contains corn on the cob cut into rounds, small flour dumplings (similar to Jamaican spinners), and root vegetables. Some versions include salt pork or pumpkin. This recipe is a straightforward home version without the salt pork, which is rarely stocked outside specialist stores.

content.text: Trinidadian corn soup is a party soup and a road-side soup: the kind of thing ladled into polystyrene cups at 2 in the morning after Carnival or at dawn on a Sunday when the market is starting up. It is thick from the split peas, fragrant from the thyme and scotch bonnet, and the corn rounds give you something to work at between the spoonfuls. The small flour dumplings, stiffer than Jamaican spinners but similar in spirit, absorb the broth.

The split peas need 45 minutes to fully break down and give the broth its body. Don't rush this step. The corn and potato go in later; the dumplings last. The whole thing is straightforward. It just takes time.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 5 minutes. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic. Add the {{split-peas-yellow}} of yellow split peas and pour over 1.5 litres of cold water. Add the spring onion bundle, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, and whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a boil, then simmer covered for 45 minutes until the split peas are completely broken down and the broth is thick.

Add the {{sweetcorn}} sweetcorn rounds and {{potato}} potatoes to the simmering soup. While they cook, make the dumplings: mix the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and enough cold water (about 50 ml) to make a very stiff dough. Pinch off small pieces and roll between your palms into short cylinders about 2 cm long. Drop the dumplings into the soup. Simmer uncovered for 15 minutes until the corn and potato are tender and the dumplings are cooked through. Remove the scotch bonnet, spring onion bundle, and thyme before serving.

items.fix: Very old dried split peas can take significantly longer. Increase to a proper simmer, not a bare blip. Older peas may need 60 to 75 minutes.
items.cause: Old split peas; heat too low
items.symptom: Split peas not breaking down after 45 minutes

items.fix: Add 200 to 300 ml of hot water and stir to loosen. The consistency should coat the back of a spoon but still pour.
items.cause: Split peas fully dissolved; not enough water

items.fix: The dumpling dough should be very stiff, much stiffer than bread dough. Add dumplings only when the soup is at a simmer, not a rolling boil.
items.cause: Dough too soft; added too early

items.fix: Corn rounds need at least 15 minutes in simmering liquid. They should offer no resistance when pierced with a knife.

content.text: Corn soup has a specific place in the Trinidadian calendar: it appears in its largest quantities at Carnival, sold from enormous pots at the side of the road through the night and into the morning. It is the food that restores you: thick, filling, and warming. At home it is a Sunday soup or a quick weekday lunch. The combination of split peas and corn is deeply Caribbean, drawing on both the African-Trinidadian and Indo-Trinidadian traditions in a single bowl.
